mission.title: Install and Configure K3s (Lightweight Kubernetes)
mission.description: K3s is a lightweight Kubernetes distribution designed for resource-constrained environments, making it ideal for edge computing, IoT, and development. It simplifies the Kubernetes experience with a single binary and minimal dependencies.

steps.title: Install K3s
steps.description:
To install K3s, run the following command on your Linux node. This installs version v1.31.0 of K3s. You can adjust the version as needed.
```bash
curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | INSTALL_K3S_VERSION=v1.31.0 sh -
```
After installation, check the status of the K3s service:
```bash
sudo systemctl status k3s
```
Verify the installation with:
```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
To configure kubectl to connect to your K3s cluster, run:
```bash
export KUBECONFIG=/etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml
``` 
Then verify the connection:
```bash
kubectl cluster-info
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
To enable the Metrics Server for monitoring, apply the following manifest:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
```
Verify that the Metrics Server is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall K3s
uninstall.description:
To uninstall K3s, run the following command:
```bash
/usr/local/bin/k3s-uninstall.sh
```
This will remove K3s and its associated resources. To clean up any remaining resources, you may also want to delete the K3s configuration file:
```bash
sudo rm -rf /etc/rancher/k3s
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade K3s
upgrade.description:
To upgrade K3s to a newer version, first check the current version:
```bash
k3s --version
```
Backup your current configuration:
```bash
sudo cp /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml.bak
```
Then, run the upgrade command, replacing 'v1.31.1' with the desired version:
```bash
curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | INSTALL_K3S_VERSION=v1.31.1 sh -
```
After upgrading, verify the installation:
```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

troubleshooting.title: K3s service not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the K3s service fails to start, check the logs:
```bash
sudo journalctl -u k3s
```
Common issues include insufficient memory or conflicting services. Ensure your system meets the minimum requirements and no other Kubernetes services are running.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl not connecting
troubleshooting.description:
If kubectl cannot connect to the cluster, ensure the KUBECONFIG environment variable is set correctly:
```bash
echo $KUBECONFIG
```
If it is not set, run:
```bash
export KUBECONFIG=/etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml
```

troubleshooting.title: Node not Ready
troubleshooting.description:
If a node shows 'NotReady', check the node conditions:
```bash
kubectl describe node <node-name>
```
Look for issues related to network connectivity or resource limits.

versionNotes.changes: Improved support for ARM architectures and enhanced security features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for certain legacy storage backends.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new CNI plugins and improved metrics server integration.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of the old K3s CLI commands.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of K3s will have a running cluster with nodes in 'Ready' state and kubectl successfully connecting to the cluster. You should also have the Metrics Server running for monitoring.

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Linux environment with curl and bash installed.
